questions.prompt: What is the actual design problem with the classic Singleton, given that many systems legitimately need exactly one instance of something?

options.text: The global access point: dependencies vanish from signatures, tests share bleeding state, and every consumer is welded to one concrete instance — the single-instance property itself is fine when owned by the composition root.
Creating only one instance wastes the memory reserved for the class.
Singletons cannot be made thread-safe.
Modern languages have removed static fields, making the pattern impossible.

questions.explanation: Separate the two properties the pattern conflates: *one instance* (often correct) and *globally reachable* (the disease). Nothing in a method signature reveals a `Config.instance()` call, so the dependency graph lies; test isolation dies because state persists across tests. Inject the single instance and both problems evaporate. (b) is meaningless. (c) thread-safe singletons are routine (holder idiom, locks). (d) is false.

questions.prompt: This Android/Flutter-style screen subscribes to a stream. What Observer failure mode does it exhibit?

options.text: A leaked subscription: the listener outlives the screen, holding it in memory and firing setState on a disposed widget — cancel the StreamSubscription in dispose().
Reentrancy: the observer mutates the observable during notification.
The stream should be a broadcast stream to allow multiple screens.
listen() is deprecated in favor of StreamBuilder in all cases.

questions.explanation: Unsubscribed observers are the #1 memory leak in UI code: the repository's listener list keeps a strong reference to the whole screen state after navigation, and late events hit a dead widget (`setState called after dispose`). Store the `StreamSubscription` and cancel it in `dispose()` — or use a widget (StreamBuilder) that manages the lifecycle. (b) nothing here writes back to the observable. (c) broadcast-vs-single is orthogonal. (d) StreamBuilder is often cleaner but `listen` is not deprecated; the flaw is the missing cancel, not the API choice.

questions.prompt: You need per-request retry, then caching, then metrics around an HTTP client — composable in different orders per environment. Which pattern is this, and why does it fit?

options.text: Decorator: each concern implements the client interface and wraps another instance, so concerns stack in any order without touching the underlying client.
Template Method: a base client with retry/cache/metrics hook methods overridden per environment.
Singleton: one preconfigured client shared globally avoids the problem.
Visitor: each concern visits the request object.

questions.explanation: Same-interface wrapping is exactly the middleware shape: `Metrics(Cache(Retry(realClient)))`, reordered by rewiring, each concern testable alone. (b) inheritance fixes the combination at compile time and hits chapter 3's fragile-base problems — one subclass per permutation. (c) dodges the requirement (different stacks per environment). (d) Visitor solves operations-over-a-node-hierarchy, not request pipelines.

questions.prompt: Which GoF patterns are (mostly) absorbed into mainstream language features, making the hand-rolled class form usually unnecessary? Select all that apply.

options.text: Iterator — foreach loops, Iterable protocols, generators
Command (simple invocation cases) — lambdas and closures
Adapter — no longer needed once SDKs publish interfaces
Prototype — copyWith / record with-expressions / structured clone

questions.explanation: (a) writing a GoF Iterator class in 2026 is museum code. (b) a lambda *is* a command object for invocation purposes; the class form earns its keep only with undo state or serialization (job queues). (d) modern copy mechanisms cover Prototype's intent. (c) is the odd one out: Adapter is *more* relevant than ever — it's how DIP keeps vendor SDK types out of your domain, and no language feature does that translation for you.

questions.prompt: A mid-level engineer proposes starting the new module design by 'choosing which patterns to use.' What is the staff-level correction?

options.text: Design for the requirements simply, then let patterns enter as refactorings when a specific pressure appears — patterns are vocabulary for shapes that emerge, not targets to aim at.
Agree, but limit the design to a maximum of three patterns.
Patterns are obsolete; the catalog should not influence design at all.
Require Factory, Strategy, and Observer in every module for consistency.

questions.explanation: Pattern-first design is how `AbstractSingletonProxyFactoryBean` happens: structure without pressure. The chapter's asymmetry seals it — adding a pattern to simple code is easy; removing one from clever code is hard, so default simple. (b) rations the mistake instead of correcting it. (c) overshoots: the vocabulary value ('decorate the client with retry') is real and permanent. (d) is cargo cult by policy.

questions.prompt: In a language with sealed classes and exhaustive pattern matching (Kotlin, Dart 3, Java 21+), when does the Visitor pattern still earn its ceremony over a switch?

options.text: Rarely — mainly when the node hierarchy can't be sealed (open for external extension) yet operations must still dispatch on concrete type, or when double-dispatch over two hierarchies is genuinely needed.
Always — Visitor is type-safe and switches are not.
Whenever there are more than five node types.
Never — Visitor should be deleted from all modern codebases on sight.

questions.explanation: Sealed types + exhaustive matching deliver Visitor's compile-time completeness guarantee with a fraction of the ceremony — that was Visitor's main selling point. What remains for Visitor: unsealed/externally-extensible hierarchies (accept/visit gives you dispatch without the compiler knowing all subtypes) and true double-dispatch. (b) inverts the modern situation — exhaustive switches over sealed types are exactly as safe. (c) node count is irrelevant. (d) overcorrects: legacy code and the named cases keep it legitimate.
